refactor: convert landing how-it-works to tsx and remove raw-html pipeline (PR3/3) (#881)

* refactor: port landing how-it-works page to tsx

HowItWorksBody 1:1 port of the docs-tier page (sidebar tab nav, six
js-populated view panels, legend) reusing BackLink and SiteFooter; css moved
verbatim with only the stale wrapper comment reworded; frozen three-file
injection order preserved. diff-dom gains colon-space normalization inside
style declarations (react ssr emits prop:value; this page's baseline is the
only one written prop: value) with a regression test. The overview lede
avoids an entity in the tag-adjacent text run — next's jsx transform drops
the leading space after an inline tag when the same run carries an entity,
which shipped as a joined word until the review's byte-level audit caught it.

test: de-flake use-extension-bridge cache-seeding assertion (#880)

The shared makeQueryClient sets gcTime 0, so the entry seeded by the
mutation's onSuccess has no observers and tanstack query's immediate
eviction timer races the test's waitFor polling (4 documented red CI
sightings, ~5% repro in isolation). Use a persistentClient (gcTime and
staleTime infinity) for that one test, mirroring the use-autopilot
suite's established pattern for observer-less cache seeding. 40/40
consecutive isolated runs green post-fix; full renderer suite green.

fix(export): keep a subject-line-first letterhead-less letter's subject line (#878)

Extends the #876 name-block guard in both docx letter renderer arms with
!is_subject_line, so a letter opening with a subject/reference line (e.g. a
German Betreff: line) and no letterhead name no longer has that line consumed

and fails on the unguarded renderer.

Fixes #877

chore: update export benchmark data [skip ci]

fix(export): do not consume a letter's salutation as the letterhead name (#876)

The DOCX letter renderers treated the first non-blank line as the letterhead
name whatever it was: `if !header_done && docx.document.children.is_empty()`.
A letter that opens straight at the salutation (no letterhead) therefore had its
"Dear …" line consumed as the name and replaced with meta.candidate_name — and
because `in_body` is set only in the salutation arm, the whole body then
rendered in the muted addressee style.

Guard the name block with `!is_salutation && !is_signoff` (both already computed
just above) so a salutation-first letter falls through to the salutation arm,
which sets header_done/in_body correctly. The block is duplicated in
generate_cover_letter_docx_classic (Classic) and generate_cover_letter_docx_layout
(Refined/Banded); both are guarded.

Test opens a letterhead-less letter with a candidate name set and asserts the
"Dear Hiring Manager" line survives, across all three layouts. Fails on main
(Classic path: the salutation is replaced by the candidate name).

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(ai-generations): make the mismatch merge follow a language-bearing save (#874)

merge_application OR-accumulated mismatch (incoming || existing) while every
other scalar is pick/latest-wins. mismatch is derived from the resume/jobAd
language pair, so OR made a true permanent: a corrected regeneration
(mismatch=false) could never clear a stale language-mismatch warning.

Follow incoming.mismatch only when the incoming save carries the language pair
(both language fields non-empty) — mirroring how the language fields themselves
merge via pick. A content-less answers/interview-only save (blank languages,
default mismatch=false) then keeps the existing verdict instead of clobbering
it, and a real correction clears it.

Regression test fails on the old code (the stale true is never cleared).

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(ui): flush a pending RichTextEditor edit on unmount instead of dropping it (#841)

* fix(ui): flush a pending RichTextEditor edit on unmount instead of dropping it

The unmount effect is commented "Flush any pending debounce on unmount so the
last edit is never lost", but its body only cleared the timer — `emit` was
never called, so the pending markdown was discarded.

`RichTextEditor` is rendered conditionally (Preview / Edit / Source in
EditableOutput), so switching view unmounts it. Type, then click Preview within
ONCHANGE_DEBOUNCE_MS (200ms) and the last edit is gone: the parent-owned
`value` still holds the pre-keystroke markdown, the preview/PDF renders stale
text, and re-entering Edit re-parses the stale value. No error, no indication.
The same loss happens on any unmount of the surrounding panel inside the
debounce window — the ordinary "type, then immediately click away" pattern.

Capture the pending document synchronously in `onUpdate` (a ProseMirror node is
immutable, so it is just a pointer copy) and serialize THAT in the cleanup,
rather than reaching into an editor Tiptap may already have destroyed by the
time this component's cleanup runs. The timer path clears the ref before
emitting, so the two paths can never double-emit.

`emitDoc` is `useCallback([])`-stable, so `emit` stays stable and the existing
`useImperativeHandle(ref, …, [editor, emit])` dependency list is unaffected.

* test(ui): make the unmount-flush suite deterministic

The new suite was red on CI at the previous commit. Two independent causes,
both in the test setup rather than the production change:

- The editor's initial-content load is itself a transaction, so a mounted
  editor schedules one debounced emit on its own. Under real timers that
  mount emit could fire before the "nothing emitted yet" assertion on a
  slower runner. Drain and clear it explicitly before the edit under test.

- jsdom implements no layout, so `Range.getClientRects` is undefined and
  ProseMirror's coordsAtPos -> singleRect throws an unhandled
  "target.getClientRects is not a function" from scrollToSelection on every
  editor transaction. Stub `Range.getClientRects`/`getBoundingClientRect`
  in the shared jsdom setup alongside the existing scrollIntoView stub, so
  the "still emits exactly once" assertion is no longer polluted by it.

fix(scraper): delegate board location filtering to the central location_filter (#835)

* fix(scraper): match a 'City, Country' location label segment-wise

The location box normally holds the geocode picker's "<city>, <country>"
label — `commands::geocoding` builds it exactly so "the UI only ever shows
'City, Country'", and both entry points (jobs ScrapeFilters, autopilot
StepTarget) write `suggestion.display` straight into `location`.

The two boards that filter location client-side required that WHOLE label to
appear as one contiguous substring of the board's own location text. No board
writes it that way:

  The Muse        "New York, NY"            vs request "New York, United States"
  GermanTechJobs  "Karl-Marx-Allee 1, Berlin" / "Munich, Bayern"
                                            vs request "Berlin, Germany"

so the filter dropped every row and the board reported 0 results for any
picked city. Typing free text without picking a suggestion still worked,
which is why it survives manual testing.

Match segment-wise instead: any non-empty comma-separated part of the request
appearing in the haystack is enough. Conservative — it errs toward keeping,
mirroring the engine's own `location_filter`, which already tokenizes the
request and keeps on any token hit for this exact reason.

- adds `common::location_matches`, shared by both call sites
- extracts `germantechjobs::matches_gtj_filters` from `search()`'s `retain`
  so tests drive the real predicate instead of a copy that could diverge
  (same reasoning as The Muse's `apply_filters`)

Single-segment requests behave exactly as before.

* fix(scraper): delegate board location filtering to the central location_filter

Reworks the fix per review. The first version added a board-local segment-wise
matcher (`location_matches`); this removes board-local LOCATION filtering from
the three `supports_location() == false` boards entirely and lets the engine's
central `location_filter` be the single authority — the reviewer's preferred
direction, and the one the issue points at.

Why this is correct and strictly better than a second local matcher:

- The Muse, Comeet and GermanTechJobs are all non-location boards, so the engine
  already runs `location_filter` on their output both as a live gate
  (`KeepItemByBoardFn`) and as a post-hoc safety net. Board-local location
  filtering was redundant with it.
- The central filter is diaeresis/exonym-aware (München/Munich, Köln/Cologne via
  the curated table + DIN-5007-2 folding) and conservative (keeps remote /
  empty / unknown, and a country-code-only request is inert). A raw
  case-insensitive board-local matcher had none of that and, running BEFORE the
  central filter, produced false drops the central filter is designed to rescue.
- The original 0-results bug is still fixed: `location_spec()` feeds the picker's
  "<city>, <country>" label into the central filter, which TOKENISES it (it does
  not require the whole label as one substring), so a geocode-picked city keeps
  its rows via the central filter alone.

Changes:
- common.rs: `matches_filters` is now keyword-only (drops the `location` param);
  `location_matches` is deleted.
- germantechjobs: `matches_gtj_filters` is keyword-only; the unused `loc` local
  is removed.
- The Muse / Comeet call sites pass query only.
- Tests updated across common/themuse/germantechjobs: the board-local location
  assertions are replaced with ones pinning that these boards no longer drop on
  location (delegated to the central filter, whose own exonym/diacritic behaviour
  is unit-tested in engine::location_filter).

Known limitation (unchanged by this PR, now consistent across all non-location
boards): because `location_spec()` stores the whole label in `city`, the central
filter also tokenises the country segment, so "Munich, Germany" keeps a
"Frankfurt, Germany" row. That is a central-filter property shared by every
non-location board, not something this PR introduces; tightening it is a separate
change to `location_filter`/`location_spec`.

fix(scraper): keep distinct Hacker News job ids apart in cross-source dedup (#845)

`identifying_query_params` allowlisted only Indeed, so `normalize_job_url`
drops the entire query string for every other host. But `boards::ycombinator`
falls back to `news.ycombinator.com/item?id=<id>` whenever an HN job story
carries no external url — the id lives in the QUERY:

    https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=44100001 -> .../item
    https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=44100002 -> .../item

so `dedup_cross_source` collapsed every text-only HN posting into one row. The
allowlist's own doc comment already stated the rule it was violating ("Keep in
sync with the canonical URL builders … that place a job id in the query
string"); ycombinator is a second such builder. The aggregator's `canonical_url`
documents the same hazard from the other direction.

Add the `news.ycombinator.com` -> `id` entry on both sides of the lockstep pair
(Rust + the TS mirror), so tracking params on the same id still collapse while
distinct ids stay apart.

Also tightens the doc comment so `applications/mod.rs` stays under the R8
1400-LOC hard cap (1399).

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(ui): ignore a superseded geocode response (#843)

The debounce in `useGeocoding` only bounds when a request STARTS. Once one is
in flight nothing cancels it, and the cleanup clears only the pending timer, so
`setSuggestions(s)` ran unconditionally on whatever resolved last.

Two requests are in flight whenever the user resumes typing after a pause
longer than the 300ms debounce — the normal rhythm for typing a city name. If
the older, slower one wins the race (Nominatim is rate-limited to ~1 req/s and
the Rust side caches, so latencies vary by seconds) it overwrites the list with
suggestions for a query the user has already typed past, and `setActiveIndex(-1)`
wipes their keyboard selection mid-interaction.

Flag the run as superseded in the cleanup and drop a late resolution. React
always runs the previous cleanup before the next effect run, so this also
covers the `< 2 chars` early-return path — which otherwise let an in-flight
request repopulate a list the hook had just cleared — and stops a setState
after unmount.

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(export): keep body paragraphs separate in a letter with no letterhead (#839)

The header-skip arm in `parse_cover_letter` was not anchored to the header:

    if skip_lines < 3
        && (clean.is_empty() || …name… || …contact echo…)

`skip_lines` only advances when the arm fires, so the arm stays armed for the
whole document until three skippable lines have accumulated. Since
`clean.is_empty()` alone satisfies it, any of the first three blank lines is
consumed with `continue` — including blank lines in the BODY, which never
reach the paragraph-flush branch below. The next body line is then space-joined
onto the un-flushed paragraph.

A letter that opens straight at the salutation therefore renders as ONE run-on
block instead of separate paragraphs. That shape is exactly what the agent
produces: `COVER_LETTER_SYSTEM` asks for the finished letter only — "no
preamble or commentary" — so there is no name/contact echo to burn the three
skips before the body starts. It is also the natural hand-edited shape, since
the template renders the letterhead from `ContactProfile` and the parser
deliberately drops any contact echo from the body.

The repo fixture `EN_LETTER` happens to spend all three skips on its
letterhead before the body, which is why the existing tests pass.

Bound the arm with `!body_started`. Safe by construction: `current_para_text`
is only appended to in the `body_started` branch, so no paragraph can be
pending while the arm is reachable. Affects both the PDF download and the live
preview (`render_letter_pdf` / `render_letter_svg_pages`).

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(ai): stop corrupting streamed text at a chunk boundary (#837)

`stream_response` decoded each transport read on its own:

    buf.push_str(&String::from_utf8_lossy(&bytes));

`reqwest::Response::chunk` splits the body at arbitrary byte offsets — a
chunked-transfer body surfaces as the socket reads land, and h2 DATA frames
are cut wherever the server flushed — so one multi-byte character routinely
straddles two reads. `from_utf8_lossy` then replaced BOTH halves with U+FFFD
and consumed the partial bytes; nothing carried the incomplete tail forward.

The corruption was silent. U+FFFD is legal inside a JSON string, so the frame
still parsed and the mojibake flowed through `emit_delta` to the live view
AND through `answer` into `finish`, which persists it as `result.text` — so
the renderer's poll fallback recovered the same corrupted document. Cover
letters routinely contain em dashes, curly quotes and accented characters,
every one of which is a 2-3 byte sequence.

This is the single streaming loop behind every cloud adapter (openai,
anthropic, gemini, ollama, and every OpenAI-compatible gateway), so it
affected all of them.

Add `push_utf8`, which holds an incomplete trailing sequence in a `carry`
buffer until the next read completes it. Genuinely invalid bytes (a corrupt
transfer, never a provider) still collapse to one U+FFFD and are skipped, so
a malformed stream can never stall the loop.

Applied at all three sites that decoded a read in isolation: the production
loop, the test-only `drive_stream` core, and `ollama::pull`'s progress
stream.

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(ui): stop rendering "0mo ago" for a 28-29 day old timestamp (#833)

useFormatRelativeTime ended the week tier at `weeks < 4` (day 28) but
floors the month tier on `days / 30`, so days 28 and 29 fell through to
`monthsAgo` with `months === 0` — a month-old job posting or generation
card rendered "0mo ago" ("vor 0 Mo." in de).

Hand over to the month tier at the same 30-day boundary `months` is
computed on, so the tiers stay contiguous: days 28-29 now render "4w ago".

Adds the hook's first unit test, including a sweep asserting no tier ever
interpolates a zero count.

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>

fix: persist completed stream text so the poll fallback works (#828)

* fix(ai): persist the completed stream text for the poll fallback

The renderer's poll fallback (awaitAiStream) recovers a dropped stream frame or a
missed `done` event by reading `result.text` from the completed job, but the backend
persisted only `{ done: true }` — so PR #802's longer-wins fix was a runtime no-op.

Accumulate the completed answer (non-thinking deltas only) in the provider-agnostic
streaming completion path (`stream::finish`, shared by openai/anthropic/gemini/ollama)
and in the CLI-agent path (`cli_agent::emit_done`), and persist it as `result.text`.
Both strip inline `<think>…</think>` via a shared `strip_think_blocks` mirroring the
renderer's think-splitter, so the persisted text is the SAME shape the renderer buffers:
the poll's `persisted.length > buffer.length` compares like-for-like and can never
resolve reasoning markup into the final document.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* test(generate): align the stream poll test with the real job contract

The prior mock returned `{ status: 'completed', result: { text } }`, a shape the
backend never produced, so the regression test proved nothing. Mirror the real
contract (`result: { done: true, text }`, text think-stripped) and add a case pinning
that a recovered persisted result can never surface `<think>` markup.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(agent): clamp confirm-request args to the called tool's own cap (#823)

ARGS_DISPLAY_CAP is the max of COVER_LETTER_CAP (20k) and SAVED_RESUME_CAP

and silently clipped on save — the user approved text that was not the text
persisted.

module's display/edit-fidelity invariant; it does not hold the "never show more"
half for whichever tool has the smaller cap.

fallback for an unknown/future tool, preserving the never-show-less guarantee by

fix(scraping): cap the body read on the user-supplied-url path (#821)

resolve_uncached called Response::text() on the generic-HTML fallback, buffering
the whole body with no size limit. That URL is attacker-influenced — which is
why the fetch goes through get_guarded_following_redirects — so a hostile or
broken host could stream an arbitrarily large body. The 20s timeout bounds how
long we read, not how much.

The board path already caps at 8 MB inside fetch_text, with a Content-Length
pre-check and a streamed accumulation that aborts mid-body. fetch_text is not a
drop-in here: it uses the shared client and would drop the SSRF guard.

Extract that logic into scraping::http::read_text_capped(response, cap) — same
charset handling, so umlauts still decode — have fetch_text call it so there is
one implementation, and read the scrape_url fallback through it at 8 MB.

Adds wiremock tests for both directions of the helper; the 40 existing
scraping::http tests cover the fetch_text refactor.

fix(credentials): never treat an unreadable metadata index as empty (#819)

load_meta mapped BOTH a read error and a parse error to unwrap_or_default() and
cached the result. Since the writer persists the whole map, the next set/remove
then wrote a one-entry file and orphaned every previously saved credential from
the index — the keychain secrets survive, but nothing can find them again.

Separately, set/remove took the cache lock inside load_meta, released it, and
retook it inside save_meta, so two concurrent writers could start from the same
snapshot and the second would drop the first's entry.

read_meta_file now separates "file absent" (empty index, first run) from
"unreadable/corrupt" (an error). load_meta still degrades to empty for read-only
callers but no longer caches that, and mutate_meta holds the lock across the
whole read-modify-write and refuses to write over an index it could not read,
leaving the user's file recoverable. save_meta is subsumed by mutate_meta.

Adds a corrupt-index test (asserting the file is left byte-for-byte intact) and
an 80-write two-thread concurrency test.

fix(ai-generations): key the per-job aggregate on the normalized url (#816)

save_application looked its row up with the raw job_url, while the sibling
ApplicationStore dedupes on normalize_job_url. So the same job reached under
different tracking params — the norm on query-id boards like Indeed — missed its
own aggregate and forked a second "one-per-job" row, scattering the resume,
cover letter and answers for one job across two records.

The mismatch is already documented in commands/ai_generations.rs:84-86 ("which
never matches for query-id boards like Indeed").

Normalize on the write path so the stored key matches ApplicationStore's, and
fall back to a raw-url lookup so a row written before this change still merges
and is migrated onto the normalized key instead of being orphaned. The fallback
is guarded so an unusable url (normalize -> "") cannot merge into an unrelated
legacy row.

AiGenerationStore::applied_job_urls has no production consumer (the applied flag
comes from ApplicationStore's, already normalized), so nothing downstream
changes shape.

and fragments. So a job re-surfacing as ...?utm_source=indeed was "new" to the
skip and "already known" to the merge: a Completer::complete call was made and

Aggregators append varying utm_* params, so this fired on essentially every run

fix(applications): read the row inside set_status's transaction (#811)

set_status called self.get(id), which takes and RELEASES the mutex, and only
then opened the write transaction. A transition committing in that gap was
invisible to the call, so the status_events row it appended recorded a
from_status the row no longer had — an append-only history containing a link
that never happened.

The transaction already existed so the UPDATE and its event row land together;
the read was just left outside it. Move it in via row_by_id_conn.

This keeps set_status an unconditional setter — transition_status_if remains
the compare-and-set path, and its doc comment already warns about exactly this
get-then-set pattern.

The new race test walks status_events by rowid (commit order; the `at` column
is millisecond-resolution and ties freely) and asserts each from_status equals
the previous to_status. It fails 3/3 against the old code and passed 5/5 with
the fix. Net +2 LOC, keeping the file under the R8 cap.

fix(generate): prefer the persisted result over a truncated stream buffer (#802)

The job-status poll resolved buffer || job.result?.text, consulting the
persisted text only when the buffer was EMPTY. That covers a lost final done
chunk, but not a lost interior delta — which leaves a truncated but truthy
prefix that short-circuits the ||, gets resolved as the finished document and is
then persisted by the caller.

The poll exists because deltas can be dropped, so the buffer is exactly the
value that cannot be assumed complete. Resolve whichever of the two is longer:
a partial stream can never beat the persisted result, and a missing result.text
still falls back to what did stream.

Same principle the timeout path already states — never persist truncated output
as success.

Adds stream-promise.test.ts; the truncated-prefix case fails on the old code.

fix(extension): reply via the response callback so popup requests work on chrome (#788)

The onMessage listener returned handleRequest(...) — a Promise. That is the
Firefox browser.* convention; Chromium's chrome.runtime.onMessage requires
sendResponse plus a LITERAL true return to keep the channel open. A Promise is
truthy but not true, so Chrome closed the channel with no reply, sendMessage
resolved undefined, and popup.ts reported "No response from the extension
background." for every request/response action.

@wxt-dev/browser does not bridge that: its whole runtime is
  export const browser = globalThis.browser?.runtime?.id ? globalThis.browser : globalThis.chrome;
with no webextension-polyfill dependency, so on Chromium browser === chrome.
One-way background->popup pushes were unaffected, so the pill could still read
"Connected" while every button errored.

sendResponse + return true is accepted by both engines. A rejection now also
answers, instead of leaving the port to time out into the same message.

background.test.ts's send() helper now drives the listener with a sendResponse
callback and asserts the literal-true return; without the fix, 62 of its 65
tests fail on that assertion.

city|town|phone|mobile were unanchored substrings in NAMED_KEY_PATTERNS, so

a negative, fractional, oversized, string or object value. That None is the same

bad-typed reminder silently CLEARED it and the call still reported success.

Every other bad input on this handler is rejected — validate_recipient_email and

fix(spend): recognise a port-less ipv6 loopback as local (#780)

is_localhost_url stripped the port via the LAST colon. An IPv6 host has colons
of its own, so for a bracketed host with no port (http://[::1]/v1) that cut the
host down to "[:" and matched nothing — and the bare "::1" arm was dead for the
same reason. Only the ported spelling worked, which is the one the test covered.

is_free_call therefore returned false for a local server, and since a local
model name is not in RATES, estimate_cost billed it at the DEFAULT_RATE
$3/$15-per-1M fallback: a fabricated cost for a free call.

Strip the port structurally instead — up to and including ] for a bracketed
host, whole for a bare IPv6, single-colon split otherwise — and extend the
existing test with the port-less forms.

fix(autopilot): honour a cancel on the scrape error path (#778)

The engine reports an early Stop as Err("scrape cancelled") — deliberately, per
its F6 comment: Err only when the user cancelled AND no items were recovered.
The command's Err arm had no cancel check, so it unconditionally called
fail_run_without_summaries + job_fail and returned {error}. The only cancel
handler sat on the Ok path and was never reached.

Two results: the UI showed Failed for a run the user deliberately stopped, and
because outcome_failed treats any {error} payload as a failure, the scheduler
re-ran the very scrape that was stopped.

handler, returning {cancelled: true} — the shape outcome_failed already treats

fix(postings): write interactions atomically and stop swallowing write errors (#776)

save() used fs::write, which truncates the target in place, and then discarded
the Result with .ok(). So a crash mid-write left interactions.json truncated,
and a failed write (read-only volume, full disk) was reported as success by
upsert/import_bundle while the record lived only in memory and vanished on the
next restart. A serialization failure was swallowed the same way.

Write to interactions.json.tmp and rename it over the target — the same trick
the corrupt-file backup path already uses — and log every failure, cleaning up
the temp file and leaving the previous file intact.

save() is called from upsert/clear_all/import_bundle, none of which return a
Result, so this logs rather than changing those public signatures.

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(documents): validate an import bundle before clearing the store (#774)

import() called clear_all() — which wipes documents, vectors, posting_vectors
and match_scores — and only then deserialized the rows one by one, with no
enclosing transaction. A malformed row at index k therefore returned Err with
the user's originals already destroyed and rows 0..k half-inserted.

Deserialize every DocumentRecord and its optional vector first, then clear and
repopulate, matching ai_generations/applications/dedup/discovered, which all
validate up-front for this exact reason.

The vector + vectorSpace reconstruction moves to parse_exported_vector() with
unchanged behaviour, including the legacy Ollama/nomic-embed-text fallback.

Adds a regression test; it fails on the old code with "the prior library must
survive a failed import: left 0, right 1".

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(applications): make update_fields read-modify-write atomic (#772)

* fix(applications): make update_fields read-modify-write atomic

update_fields called self.get(id) — which takes and RELEASES the mutex — then
self.write_row(&app), which retakes it. write_row_conn is a full-column upsert,
so it re-persisted every column from the stale snapshot and silently clobbered
any writer that committed in the gap: the extension's answers.save/merge_answers
or a concurrent set_status.

Fold the lookup and the write into one transaction via row_by_id_conn, mirroring
upsert_internal, which already carries this exact fix.

The new race test fails on every run against the old structure ("merge_answers
entry N was lost to a concurrent update_fields") and passed 5/5 with the fix.

* fix(applications): hold one lock instead of opening a transaction

CI caught two problems with the first version of this fix.

R8 (no oversized modules) failed: applications/mod.rs is 1398 LOC upstream
against a 1400 hard cap, and the transaction version pushed it to 1407.

Clippy -D warnings failed too: replacing the self.write_row(&app) call left
write_row itself dead.

Both point the same way — the transaction was more machinery than the fix
needs. update_fields does ONE read and ONE write, and every writer shares a
single Connection behind this mutex, so holding the lock across both is what
actually prevents the lost update; the transaction only added atomicity that a
single upsert already has.

Take the lock once, use the conn-scoped row_by_id_conn/write_row_conn, and drop
the now-unused write_row. Net effect on the file is -3 LOC (1395), so it no
longer sits against the cap.

Verified: race test passes 5/5 with the fix and fails against upstream's
update_fields; cargo test --lib 2690 passed; architecture 11 passed; clippy
--all-targets --all-features -D warnings clean; fmt clean.

fix(scraping): read the remoteok id per json variant (#770)

The id arrives as a JSON number for some rows and a JSON string for others, so
it is typed Option<serde_json::Value>. Value::to_string() JSON-serialises,
which means Value::String("1234567") became "\"1234567\"" — quotes included —
and those quotes ended up inside posting.id (remoteok:"1234567") and
external_id, corrupting the stable PostingsCache key used for dedup and
interaction tracking. Only the Number variant serialised cleanly.

Extract normalize_id() and read the value per variant. Non-string/non-integer
values still map to an empty string, so the caller's is_empty() guard drops the
row unchanged.

The pre-existing tests only exercised a numeric id, which is exactly the
variant that escapes the bug.

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(scraping): advertise only gzip on the linkedin client (#768)

The client sent `Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br`, but reqwest is built with
default-features = false and no gzip/brotli/deflate feature, so it never
auto-decompresses — and get_html decodes only the gzip magic bytes by hand.
A br/deflate response therefore reached String::from_utf8 as compressed bytes
and returned AppError::Parse("response was not valid UTF-8"), which propagates
out of search_guest page 0 and empties the whole LinkedIn board.

Advertise only gzip, and add a test pinning the header to what the decoder can
actually handle.

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>
Co-authored-by: Saeed Kolivand <51081940+saeedkolivand@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(extraction): stop rtf hex escape from slicing a str mid-character (#766)

rtf_to_text decoded \'xx via &rtf[i + 2..i + 4]. `rtf` is a Cow<str> and `i` is
a BYTE index, so that slice panics unless both ends land on UTF-8 char
boundaries — and the only guard was a length check (i + 3 < n). An RTF with
\' followed by a multi-byte character (e.g. \'€) therefore panicked with
"byte index N is not a char boundary", aborting extraction of the résumé.

Read the two digits from the `bytes` slice already in scope, validating them
as ASCII hex first. A malformed escape now advances past just the \' so the
following character's bytes stay intact; the truncated-at-end branch is
unchanged.

Adds regression tests for the multi-byte, truncated and well-formed cases.

Co-authored-by: thejesh23 <thejesh23@users.noreply.github.com>
Co-authored-by: Saeed Kolivand <51081940+saeedkolivand@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(extraction): do not emit bare w:p docx paragraphs twice (#764)

* fix(extraction): do not emit bare w:p docx paragraphs twice

parse_document chained xml.split("<w:p ") with xml.split("<w:p>"). For a
document that uses the bare <w:p> spelling (Google Docs, LibreOffice,
python-docx) the first split matches nothing and yields the WHOLE document as
one element — which parse_paragraph mines into a single boundary-less blob —
while the chained second split also yields every paragraph. All text was
emitted twice: once mashed together, once per paragraph. MS Word escaped it
only because its w:rsidR attributes leave the <w:p>-split branch empty.

Scan for the <w:p tag (requiring ' ' or '>' after it, so <w:pPr>/<w:pict> are
not mistaken for paragraphs) instead of splitting on one spelling; documents
mixing both spellings are now handled too.

Adds four regression tests that assert the EXACT extracted text — the existing
coverage used contains(), which stays green through duplication.

* test(extraction): pin self-closing w:p paragraph handling

Review follow-up on #764.

A self-closing <w:p/> is not treated as a paragraph start, so it folds into the
previous slice. That is harmless — it carries no runs, so it contributes no
text, and the old chained splits ignored it too — but the boundary was
undocumented and untested. Pin it with a test and note it in the doc comment.

Also inlines the single as_bytes() read, dropping the local binding.
